intro: The year is 1927. It's the roaring twenties - an age of jazz, prohibition, and industrial power.  You push open the door to a long-abandoned newsroom in downtown Chicago. Visible faintly through the glow of a flickering bulb, the room is enveloped in a thin veil of dust. Sprawled across desks and pooling onto the floor are various newspaper articles, letters, and typewriters frozen mid-sentence. Though you are keenly aware of how much you are NOT supposed to be in this room, strewn across the newspapers are scathing headlines that do everything to catch your eye. Mayors running speakeasies, covered-up murders of famous musicians. This was the office of Gordon Parker, a newspaper editor and political whistleblower who vanished after claiming to uncover a government cover-up tied to the city's booming industries. Files, telegrams, coded notes. Time may have erased Gordon Parker's name, but not his truth-if you're willing to search for it.

phrasechallenges.question:
Looking across the cluttered desk, you find a torn newspaper dated March 3, 1927
Headline: 'Mayor Declares Crackdown on Booze Rings.'
Scrawled beneath it in red ink:
'Im outlawed by day, poured by night, and found beneath the floorboards.'
hints: Rum, Vodka, Tequila
It's illegal during Prohibition
phrasechallenges.postQuestion:
You find a note tucked in the fold:
'Follow the bottle to its source' as well as a reference guide for a 'Caeser Cipher'
You got a new Item: Caesar Guide

item.name: Caesar Guide
item.description: A guide for how to complete a Caesar cipher.
item.useCase:
Take the string ABC, if we want to incorporate a cipher shift of +1, it would then translate as the following:
A B C ->
B C D
For a shift of +2:
A B C->
C D E

phrasechallenges.question:
You feel a persistent itch against your hip. At first, you dismiss it as dust from the newsroom, but the sensation only grows sharper — like something small is poking you through the fabric. Annoyed, you reach into your pocket and pull out a folded scrap of paper you don’t remember putting there.The note reads:“Hidden truths speak softly. Look at the start, not the end.” 
Below the message are four handwritten words: Silent – Archive – Lies – Everywhere The writing matches the style you've seen in Parker’s other notes — rushed, but deliberate. 
Using the clue on the note, what four-letter phrase do the words reveal?
hints: The note says to look at the start of each word.
Take the first letter from each of the four words.
Read them in order to reveal a four-letter phrase.
phrasechallenges.postQuestion:
As you tuck the note away, you notice another slip of paper folded behind it. It’s much older and worn, with only a single penciled mark across the corner — as if it was torn from something larger. 
Whatever it once belonged to, Parker clearly thought it mattered. You get a new item: Paper Fragment.
You got a new Item:  Itching Pocket Note

item.name: Itching Pocket Note
item.description: A worn, torn piece of paper with a single faint diagonal pencil mark across the corner. Whatever it belonged to is missing.
item.useCase: The fragment looks like part of a larger document. Finding its matching piece may reveal something important.

phrasechallenges.question:
Looking further around the desk you spot a headline reading:
'THE NOBLE EXPERIMENT'
with a political cartoon containing liquor bottles and Uncle Sam below.
Written below: 'They outlawed one vice to hide another.'
True or false: The 18th amendment in 1920 began prohibition:
hints: This short lived amendment was very controversial
Instigated the start of 'Speakeasies'
It was later repealed by the 21st amendment
phrasechallenges.postQuestion:
A paperclip holding the the newspaper together slips out of place. Attached to it is a torn photograph of the mayor outside a jazz club.
You got a new Item:Photograph

item.name: Torn Photograph
item.description:
The photo roughly shows a jazz band playing in front of night club, with the name of the band listed as [BLUE M-] - before being cut off 
On the backside of the photo is written, 'my favorite jazz band'.
item.useCase:
You hold up the photograph, the cut off name of the band still reads:
[BLUE M-]
The note on the back seems to suggest that the band name is of some importance....But where might you find the other half of the name?

phrasechallenges.question:
Behind the pamphlet is a torn telegram:
Qeb kbuq zirb fp fk obzloap
The telegram seemed to be encoded with some sort of cipher, attached is an ad:
[CAESARS PIZZERIA] with the phone number: 281-330-8004. With the first 3 in the phone number underlined.
[Enter the decoded text below:]
phrasechallenges.answer: The next clue is in records
hints: Caesar Cipher
Use a caesar cipher with a +3 shift
Once decoded, the sentence will clearly point you toward your next location.
phrasechallenges.postQuestion: You notice a filing cabinet labeled RECORDS - BLUE CANARY CLUB.

phrasechallenges.question:
Inside the RECORDS cabinet are payroll sheets and a speakeasy poster with a tear in it reading
‘The Blue Canary – Live Tonight!’ Performing:[-E MIRAGE]
 The first half of the band name seems to be missing...
hints: Check your items for something useful
The name of the band is in the 'Photograph' item
The missing word matches the part still visible in your torn photograph.
phrasechallenges.postQuestion:
Skimming through the record cabinet for the 'Blue Mirage' band, sticking out of its record sleeve appears to be an article

It's an unfinished draft of an article titled: [CHICAGO MAYOR EXPOSED FOR COLLUSION],
it seems like Parker was in the middle of writing until it abruptly stops… 

The last line written reads sloppily as : 'uNdeR the dEskk iS… '

phrasechallenges.question:
Frantically making your way back towards Parkers desk, you see below; a generic looking envelope you previously overlooked.
Appearing to be just an unanswered utility bill the envelopes return address is labeled 'NRG Energy'.
Nothing would otherwise indicate its importance,
However…
scrawled on the corner of the envelope, in small print, is the message:
Ocz adivg zqdyzixz dn czmz
Assuming it to be yet another cipher, you see in faint ink beside it:, '+5'
hints: Caesar Cipher
Use a caesar cipher with +5 shift
When shifted correctly, the message reveals what Parker desperately wanted someone to discover.
phrasechallenges.postQuestion:
Opening the plainly marked envelope, inside you find various documents. 
Photo evidence linking the running mayor to illegal drug deals with the speakeasy.
Phone logs of illicit transactions between musicians and the mayor, 
And Lastly: A a signed contract linking Mayor Whitmore to liquor shipments 
and the Deed of the illegal night Speakeasy.

Epilogue:

 Having the smoking gun to Parkers unfinished article, you decide to expose the truth.
The signatures from the mayor himself tying him to the Speakeasy, photos of him and the Jazz players in obviously illicit scenarios. The Whitmore dynasty collapses, and Parker’s story finally runs on the front page.
Chicago celebrates you as the journalist who finished what Gordon started.

phrasechallenges.question: She sells sea shells by the sea shore?
phrasechallenges.answer: She selled sea shells at the sea shore.
hints: Use your brain.

phrasechallenges.question: What has keys but no locks?
hints: Think about what you're using right now.

phrasechallenges.question: I speak without a mouth and hear without ears. I have no body, but come alive with wind. What am I?
hints: Listen carefully in the dark forest.

phrasechallenges.question: The more of me you take, the more you leave behind. What am I?
hints: Look down at the ground.

phrasechallenges.question: I have cities, but no houses. I have mountains, but no trees. I have water, but no fish. What am I?
hints: Navigate your way to the answer.
